newHtml:
Angels curse a wife who refuses sex until morning Women Prophetic Character Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #2141 (and parallels)
"If a man calls his wife to bed and she refuses, and he spends the night angry at her, the angels curse her until the next morning."

What the hadith says

When a husband wants sex and his wife refuses — for any reason — the angels of God curse her throughout the night until dawn.

Why this is a problem

  1. It eliminates marital consent as a category. Tiredness, illness, emotional distress — none are recognized. The only morally permitted answer is yes.
  2. It weaponizes metaphysics against women. The punishment is celestial, not human. Angels — the messengers of the Creator — are invoked as enforcers of a husband's sexual access.
  3. The husband's mood is the trigger. The curse is conditional on his anger. If he shrugs, no curse. If he sulks, she is cursed. Her standing with heaven depends on his emotional regulation.
  4. It is irreconcilable with any meaningful theology of consent. Modern Islamic apologists often argue marital rape is forbidden in Islam. This hadith directly contradicts that by attaching celestial punishment to non-consent. Both claims cannot be simultaneously true.

The Muslim response

Apologists argue the hadith encourages mutual consideration rather than mandating coercive compliance — a wife's obligation is to be responsive to her husband's needs, and the hadith's force is moral encouragement within a framework of mutual rights. The canonical tradition also preserves hadiths instructing husbands to be considerate, making this one piece of a balanced marital ethic.

Why it fails

The "balanced marital ethic" framing requires reading the hadith against its plain grammar. The text imposes a supernatural penalty on the wife for refusal — there is no equivalent curse on a husband who fails to be considerate. The asymmetry is the finding: one party's preference is enforced by angelic curse, the other party's needs are addressed by advisory hadith only. A system in which divine agents enforce the husband's access but not the wife's body-autonomy is not a balanced marital ethic — it is a one-way enforcement mechanism dressed as mutual obligation.

"Don't beat your wife like you beat your slave girl" — the telling analogy Women Prophetic Character Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #142
"...And do not hit your wife like one of you beats his slave girls."

What the hadith says

Husbands are instructed not to beat their wives the way they beat their slave girls. The comparison only works if beating slave girls is the assumed baseline — a practice the hadith takes for granted.

Why this is a problem

  1. It normalizes slave-beating. The reform is that wives should not receive the slave-grade beating. Slave girls still get the full beating. The hadith spares the wife while leaving the slave exactly where she was.
  2. It is a differential cruelty rule, not an abolition. The slave-girl beating is the reference object. The ruling concedes the regular practice without critiquing it.
  3. It presupposes widespread household violence against enslaved women. The rhetorical comparison only lands if every man in the audience could picture what "beating his slave girls" looked like. The hadith documents, without comment, that this was the normal experience of enslaved women in Muhammad's community.
  4. Modern translations sometimes soften it. Some English renderings replace "slave girl" with "servant" or "maid." The original Arabic is unambiguous. The euphemism tracks modern embarrassment — but the text is what it is.

The Muslim response

Apologists frame the hadith as a Quranic-era reform: in a culture where wife-beating was ordinary, the Prophet's instruction not to strike the wife as severely as a slave introduced relative restraint, with the long-term trajectory (supported by other hadith discouraging striking altogether) pointing toward non-violence. The hadith is evidence of graduated reform within a patriarchal society, not an endorsement of slave-beating.

Why it fails

The "graduated reform" framing concedes that the ethics are cultural-historical rather than eternal. The hadith's structure is a differential cruelty rule: the wife is granted a concession; the slave girl is the unchanged reference point. The text does not say "do not beat anyone" — it says do not beat your wife like you beat the slave girl, which leaves the baseline beating of slaves untouched. A reform that spares one class by reinforcing the reference status of another is not abolition; it is the restructuring of cruelty. The trajectory toward non-violence is apologetic retroactive reading — fourteen centuries of Islamic jurisprudence did not read this tradition as implicitly prohibiting slave-beating.

Abu Dawud's chapter titled "Regarding Intercourse With Captives" Women Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud Book of Marriage, Chapter 43/44
[Chapter heading] "Regarding Intercourse With Captives" [Abu Dawud Book 12, Chapter 43/44, containing rulings derived from Q 4:24 "...except those your right hand possesses"]

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud — a collection focused on legal hadiths — dedicates a named chapter to the rules of sexual intercourse with female captives. The chapter treats this topic at the same rhetorical level as rules for ablution.

Why this is a problem

  1. The category exists. Whatever the individual hadiths say, the fact that Islamic fiqh required a chapter on this subject is the finding. Captive women were a standing sexual category in Muslim military life.
  2. The Quran authorizes the category. Q 4:24 permits sex with "those your right hand possesses." Q 23:5-6 and 70:29-30 repeat the exemption. The hadith chapter is the implementation manual for verses the apologist cannot disown.
  3. It applied to married captive women. Q 4:24 explicitly overrides the prohibition on married women in the case of captives. The chapter's rulings govern sex with women whose husbands were alive and had simply lost the battle.
  4. It has been cited in the 21st century. ISIS cited exactly these hadiths and Quranic verses to justify its Yazidi slave-rape program in 2014. Any defense of the hadith corpus must account for this.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics argues the chapter regulates a practice that existed in the ancient world universally, and the regulations were protective — setting rules on waiting periods before intercourse, prohibitions on separating mothers from children, and encouraging manumission. The Quranic and hadith framework progressively dignified captive women's status within the constraints of the era.

Why it fails

Regulation is not protection when the regulated act is non-consensual sex with enslaved women. The "compared to other ancient cultures" defense concedes the moral point: the practice was wrong. A chapter on how to have sex with your captives is a chapter ratifying the category of captive-rape, regardless of which procedural guards surround it. The ISIS application is not a misreading — it is a straightforward deployment of what the chapter says. An ethics that needs rules for intercourse with captives has already conceded the practice.

Allah's Throne rests on eight angelic mountain goats above seven heavens Science Claims Strange / Obscure Strong Abu Dawud #4723, #4726
"Then above that there are eight mountain goats. The distance between their hooves and their knees is like the distance between one heaven and the next. Then on their backs is the Throne... Then Allah is above that..."

"Allah is above His Throne, and His Throne is above His heavens... and it creaks on account of Him, as the saddle creaks on account of its rider."

What the hadith says

The universe consists of seven stacked heavens. Above them are eight enormous mountain goats. On the goats' backs is Allah's Throne. On the Throne is Allah. The Throne creaks audibly, like a saddle under a heavy rider.

Why this is a problem

  1. It is a physical cosmology that modern astronomy has retired. There are no seven stacked heavens, no supporting angelic goats, no creaking throne. Each element is a Bronze Age cosmological picture, preserved intact in sahih-grade hadith.
  2. The creaking Throne implies weight and physics. "Creaks on account of Him, as the saddle creaks on account of its rider" requires Allah to have mass and to rest on a load-bearing structure — directly in tension with Q 42:11: "there is nothing like unto Him."
  3. Every apologetic exit costs something. Read literally: a medieval cosmology that is plainly false. Read metaphorically: sahih hadith speaks in fantasy imagery about the structure of the universe. Reject authenticity: you undermine the collection's authority. No path is comfortable.
  4. It drove centuries of intra-Sunni theological dispute. Hanbali scholars affirmed that Allah is literally above the heavens on the basis of hadiths like this. Ash'arites denied it. The dispute over whether God has spatial location traces directly to this text.

The Muslim response

The Athari (Hanbali-Salafi) school reads the hadith literally, affirming Allah's literal aboveness bila kayf (without asking how). The Ash'ari majority reads the imagery as metaphorical or as descriptions of divine majesty using human-scale concepts, noting that the Quran's "nothing is like unto Him" (42:11) must govern literal spatial readings. The goat imagery is understood as indicating the immensity and majesty of creation beneath the Throne.

Why it fails

The metaphorical reading is a post-hoc rescue, not the canonical hermeneutic. The hadith is preserved in sahih collections precisely because it was taken to describe actual cosmological reality. If the imagery is metaphorical, the tradition has no anchor for which other hadith descriptions of Allah and the cosmos are literal — the metaphor-rescue, applied consistently, dissolves the corpus's cosmological content entirely. A revelation that requires a thousand years of theological debate to determine whether God sits on a goat-supported throne is not a revelation that spoke clearly on its most basic subject.

The "stoning verse" admitted missing from the Quran Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #4418 (see also Bukhari 6580, #817)
"We used to recite: 'If an old man and an old woman commit adultery, stone them to death...' But the people said: 'We do not find the Verse of stoning in the Book of Allah.'"

What the hadith says

Umar and other companions testify that a verse prescribing stoning for adultery was originally part of the Quranic revelation. The verse is not in the present Quran. Umar specifically worries that future generations will reject stoning because they cannot find the verse.

Why this is a problem

  1. It contradicts the Quran's preservation doctrine. Q 15:9: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." A verse the earliest companions remembered reciting is no longer in the text. Either the preservation promise failed, or the companions' memory was wrong — and the tradition preserves them asserting it was not wrong.
  2. Stoning has no Quranic basis after the verse's disappearance. The current Quran at Q 24:2 prescribes 100 lashes for adultery, not stoning. Classical law practices stoning anyway, citing hadiths that claim to report a verse no longer in the Quran — a capital punishment resting on the authority of a missing text.
  3. Umar's anxiety is precisely that the punishment will be lost. The hadith preserves his exact worry: that future Muslims, not finding the verse, will abandon stoning. They did not — which means the practice survived the verse's erasure, a strange path for divine law to take.
  4. It is a foundational problem for Quranic inerrancy. Islamic apologetics emphasizes the Quran's perfect preservation. This hadith — graded reliably and preserved in multiple collections — says a specific legal verse fell out. Pick whichever you want to be true; the other collapses.

The Muslim response

Apologists argue the stoning verse's wording was abrogated textually while its ruling was preserved — a recognized category in Islamic jurisprudence (naskh al-tilawah duna al-hukm). The ruling is therefore operative by Prophetic sunnah, not by Quranic text. Umar's concern was practical, not theological: he wanted to prevent future doubt, not to signal a preservation failure.

Why it fails

"Abrogated in wording, retained in ruling" is the classical apologetic framework, but it creates an uncomfortable result: a capital punishment operative in Islamic law whose Quranic basis was deliberately removed. The Quran's preservation guarantee (15:9) is typically deployed to claim the text is complete and perfectly transmitted — the abrogation doctrine selectively abandons that completeness for this verse while defending it everywhere else. More fundamentally, Q 24:2 prescribes lashes for adultery with no mention of stoning. Two contradictory hudud penalties for the same offense — one in the text, one in a hadith claiming a lost verse — cannot both be divinely ordained without admitting the legal system was being made up as it went.

Safiyyah's "dowry" was her own emancipation Women Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Abu Dawud #2054
"The Prophet freed Safiyyah, and made that (emancipation) her dowry."

What the hadith says

Safiyyah was a Jewish noblewoman captured at Khaybar, whose father and husband were killed in the campaign. Muhammad freed her and "married" her — with her own emancipation from the slavery he imposed functioning as the mahr (dowry).

Why this is a problem

  1. Mahr is supposed to be the groom's gift to the bride. Here, Muhammad "gave" Safiyyah her own freedom — a right he controlled. Ending an injustice you are imposing is not a wedding present.
  2. Consent is structurally impossible. Safiyyah had just seen her male relatives killed. She was offered freedom contingent on marrying Muhammad the same day. To refuse was to remain a slave. That is not a proposal.
  3. The ruling became precedent. Abu Dawud preserves this in the Book of Marriage. Future Muslim masters could free their slave-women and call the emancipation the mahr. The Safiyyah case was a regularized legal template.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics frames this as an honorable act: rather than treating Safiyyah as ordinary war-booty, Muhammad elevated her to wife-status with her freedom as the bridal-gift, preserving her dignity. Modern apologists emphasize Safiyyah's later devout-Muslim identity and her honored status as Mother of the Believers.

Why it fails

Elevating one woman from war-booty to wife-status leaves the war-booty framework fully operational for everyone else — Safiyyah's special honor presupposes the ordinary slavery the rest of the Khaybar women experienced. The "she became devout" argument addresses post-marriage outcomes, not the consent question at the moment of marriage. The "freedom as mahr" device is legally clever and morally incoherent: the same man who imposed the captivity removes it as a gift. Under any framework that takes consent seriously, a proposal whose alternative is continued enslavement is coercion.

Abu Dawud's chapter: "How Were the Jews Expelled from Al-Madinah?" Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Abu Dawud Book 14, Chapter 21/22
[Chapter title:] "How Were the Jews Expelled from Al-Madinah?"

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud devotes a dedicated chapter to documenting the expulsion of the Jewish tribes of Medina and ultimately the Arabian peninsula, cataloging Muhammad's role and the procedures used.

Why this is a problem

  1. The chapter heading concedes the fact. The Jews were expelled. The hadith collection asks only how, not whether the expulsion was just.
  2. It is the textual basis for the Arabia-is-Muslim-exclusive principle. Saudi Arabia's modern policy forbidding non-Muslim worship in the Hijaz traces partly to this expulsion precedent.
  3. It collides with the Quranic framing of People of the Book. Q 5:5 permits Muslim men to marry Jewish and Christian women. The physical expulsion of those same communities is never harmonized with this inclusion.
  4. The precedent has been reactivated repeatedly. From Umayyad expulsions through 20th-century expulsions of Jews from Arab countries, the "Jews out of Arabia" pattern has drawn on this textual anchor.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics holds the expulsions were responses to specific treaty breaches by individual tribal groups in the context of active war — not a general anti-Jewish policy. The Qurayza case was adjudicated by Sa'd ibn Mu'adh applying Jewish scriptural law to a tribe that had allied with besieging enemies. The chapter catalogs events without endorsing an exclusion principle.

Why it fails

The "specific breaches" framing works case-by-case but collapses when viewed cumulatively: three Jewish tribal groups were expelled or massacred within a few years, with the result that Medina was entirely cleared of its Jewish population. The chapter heading's neutrality is the tell — the tradition's organizing question is "by what method" not "whether." A tradition whose organizing question about a community's removal is procedural has already accepted removal as the conclusion. Contemporary Saudi state policy and fourteen centuries of Islamic jurisprudence have applied the exclusion principle, not the case-by-case exception framing.

"Do not force your slave girls [into prostitution]" — and the implied baseline Women Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud #3454 (echoing Q 24:33)
"...do not force your slave girls..."

What the hadith says

The ruling — echoing Quran 24:33 — is that masters should not force their slave women into prostitution for financial gain. The master's own sexual access remains untouched.

Why this is a problem

  1. The reform presupposes the practice. The existence of the ruling proves that masters were forcing slave women into prostitution often enough to require a prophetic prohibition.
  2. The master's sexual access is not touched. The ruling restricts pimping — sending a slave woman to be used by others. It says nothing against the master's personal sexual use, which Q 4:24 explicitly permits.
  3. Q 24:33 adds: "if they want chastity." The Quran's formula is "do not force them into prostitution if they want to preserve their chastity." The conditional is damning: if the slave woman does not "want chastity," the prohibition lapses. Divine protection for an enslaved woman depends on her stated preference — preference that cannot be meaningfully stated under conditions of total power asymmetry.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics argues this prohibition shows Islam progressively restricted and dignified the position of enslaved women, establishing their right to refuse being prostituted — a protection absent in many contemporary legal systems. The trajectory was toward manumission and marriage as the preferred outcomes.

Why it fails

A moral advance that says "do not force your slave women into prostitution unless they consent" is not a moral advance — it is a protocol for slavers. The "if they want chastity" conditional is the structural problem: it makes divine protection of an enslaved woman's body depend on her own expressed preference in a context where no preference is free. The reform cleans up the worst edge of the practice while leaving the practice — including the master's unrestricted personal sexual access — entirely intact. The trajectory-to-abolition framing is retrospective; no classical jurist read these texts as implying eventual prohibition of concubinage.

The Uraniyyin — hands cut, eyes branded with heated nails, denied water Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Abu Dawud #4364, #4365
"He ordered that their hands and feet be cut off and their eyes be branded, then they were thrown in the Harrah where they asked for water but were not given any." (Sahih)

"He ordered that nails be heated, then he blinded them and cut off their hands and feet, and he did not cauterize them." (Sahih)

What the hadith says

A tribal group came to Medina, converted, got sick, was sent to drink camel urine and milk. Once well, they apostatized, murdered the herdsman, and stole the camels. Muhammad's sentence: cut off hands and feet, blind them with heated iron nails — deliberately without cauterization to maximize pain — then leave them in the volcanic desert to die of thirst.

Why this is a problem

  1. The torture precedes death and goes beyond the standard penalty. Islamic law prescribes cross-amputation or execution for highway robbery — not both plus blinding plus dehydration. Muhammad's sentence exceeded even the prescribed penalty.
  2. Omitting cauterization was deliberate. The second narration specifies the nails were heated but cauterization was omitted — the detail that normally seals the wound. Maximizing suffering was the stated purpose.
  3. Water was refused as part of the punishment. The text specifies they "asked for water but were not given any." The thirst in the volcanic desert was the killing mechanism, deliberately maintained.
  4. Even the cited Quran verse (5:33) doesn't authorize this. Q 5:33 prescribes cross-amputation or exile or crucifixion — not blinding with heated nails or death by thirst. The Prophet's penalty exceeded even the Quranic warrant offered in its defense.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics cites the principle of retaliation in kind (qisas): the Uraniyyin tortured and murdered the herdsman, so the punishment mirrored the crime. Muhammad's later hadith prohibiting mutilation is read as superseding this incident. Some scholars note the chain has grading variations that complicate its direct application as normative Sunnah.

Why it fails

The "mirror-punishment" defense fails because the hadith records deliberate maximization of suffering — nails heated, cauterization withheld, water denied. That is not proportionate retaliation; it is systematic cruelty. Muhammad's own hadith elsewhere forbids mutilation and burning, creating an internal contradiction the tradition has never cleanly resolved. The "superseded by later hadith" argument requires choosing which hadith governs; fourteen centuries of scholarship have not reached consensus, and the Abu Dawud version remains in the canonical record as sahih. The canonical record is what it is: Muhammad ordered torture and slow death by thirst.

The poisoned sheep — and Muhammad's multi-year illness Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Abu Dawud #4508, #4509, #4510
"A Jewish woman brought a poisoned sheep (meat) to the Messenger of Allah, and he ate some of it... He asked her about that, and she said: 'I wanted to kill you.' He said: 'Allah would never give you the power to do that'... And I always found it (the effect of that poison) in the uvula of the Messenger of Allah."

What the hadith says

A Jewish woman from Khaybar served Muhammad a poisoned sheep. He ate, questioned her, and she confessed the attempt. He reportedly declined to execute her. Multiple hadiths record that the poison's effect persisted in his throat for the rest of his life, and that he attributed his final illness to it.

Why this is a problem

  1. "Allah would never give you the power" is immediately falsified. Muhammad reports that the poison did affect him for years and contributed to his death. The declaration of divine protection did not hold.
  2. The poisoner's motive was political, not malicious caprice. The Jews of Khaybar had just been conquered, their men killed, their women enslaved. The poisoning attempt is what people do when they have no recourse. The hadith records the act without acknowledging that context.
  3. The parallel narrations contradict on her fate. #4509 says he did not punish her; Bukhari parallels record her executed after a companion died from the same meal. The tradition cannot agree on whether she lived or died.
  4. It undermines prophetic invulnerability. Muhammad is said elsewhere to be divinely protected from human harm. The poison got through and caused a years-long injury. The theology of prophetic protection has to absorb a multi-year poisoning.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics emphasizes the miraculous element: the meat reportedly spoke to Muhammad, warning him of the poison, allowing him to limit consumption. The lingering effects are framed as evidence of his human nature — prophets suffer like other humans, confirming his mortality. The episode is read as illustrating both prophetic insight and human vulnerability.

Why it fails

A miraculous warning that arrived too late to prevent ingestion is a miracle that didn't work. The hadith presents Muhammad's companion eating the meat and dying, while Muhammad survives with lasting effects — divine protection is partial and selectively operative. The "Allah would never give them power over you" promise is then in direct tension with a multi-year poison injury caused by exactly the human actors the protection was supposed to prevent. The episode documents precisely the failure mode a skeptical reader would expect from a human prophet told through a hagiographic lens that cannot quite absorb the facts it preserves.

Chapter: "Abusing And Beating A Captive (And Confession)" Women Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud Book 14, Chapter 117
[Chapter heading:] "Regarding Abusing And Beating A Captive, (And Confession)"

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud has a named chapter on beating and abusing captives to extract confessions. The chapter's existence signals that this was a sufficiently standard practice to require legal regulation.

Why this is a problem

  1. Extracting confession under beating is torture. Modern law categorizes "beating to extract confession" as torture whose products are inadmissible. Abu Dawud places it under legal regulation, not prohibition.
  2. The parenthetical "(And Confession)" is damning. It signals the beating is forensically oriented — coercing a desired outcome, not punishing a proven offense.
  3. Modern Muslim spokespeople frequently assert Islam forbids torture. Abu Dawud's chapter heading stands in direct contradiction. Either the heading means something other than what it says, or the claim is at odds with the classical source.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics argues the chapter regulates what happens in a specific legal context — captives taken in warfare were subject to certain interrogation procedures that the chapter placed under Prophetic constraint. The presence of regulation is itself the protection: the chapter limits what can be done, preventing worse abuses that prevailed in the ancient world.

Why it fails

Regulation of an abuse is not the same as prohibition of it. A chapter titled "Beating A Captive (And Confession)" is not limiting abuse by naming it — it is legitimizing it by categorizing it as a legal topic with proper procedures. The silent evidence of a hadith collection's chapter headings is its most candid disclosure: Abu Dawud did not title this chapter "On the Prohibition of Abusing Captives" — he titled it on the procedure for doing so. The category's existence is the problem.

"To Kill A Captive With An Arrow" — Abu Dawud's chapter title Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud Book 14, Chapter 120
[Chapter heading:] "To Kill A Captive With An Arrow"

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud dedicates a chapter to the permissibility of killing a captive by arrow rather than by sword (the default), containing hadiths affirming the method.

Why this is a problem

  1. Arrow-execution of a bound captive is target practice. The captive cannot defend himself, cannot flee. The dignity of even an unjust execution is removed — this is closer to sport than justice.
  2. The chapter's existence signals acceptance, not debate. A Book-of-Jihad chapter titled "Kill A Captive With An Arrow" is a ruling, not a question. The tradition has concluded the practice is permissible.
  3. Combined with Chapter 117's beating-for-confession, it completes a jurisprudence of captive humiliation. The captive is subject to interrogation torture, then arrow-death. The catalog is comprehensive.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics notes that the chapter addresses specific military-legal contexts — captives taken in active warfare who had committed capital offenses, where the method of execution was a secondary procedural question. The broader jurisprudence required legitimate grounds for execution before method could be considered.

Why it fails

The procedural framing describes the jurisprudential structure but does not address the moral problem: a universal ethics does not need a chapter on how to execute tied-up captives efficiently. The chapter's existence presupposes that tied captives are a regular population requiring execution-method guidance. That is not a secondary procedural question — it is a foundational moral one. The fact that classical jurisprudence required a procedure before permitting the execution does not redeem an ethics that endorsed the category in the first place.

"Do not go to extremes in cutting" — Abu Dawud's female circumcision hadith Women Prophetic Character Strange / Obscure Strong Abu Dawud #5271
"A woman used to circumcise females in Al-Madinah, and the Prophet said to her: 'Do not go to extremes in cutting, for that is better for the woman and more liked by the husband.'" (Abu Dawud grades it Da'if but preserves it; many Shafi'i jurists consider it binding.)

What the hadith says

Female circumcision was practiced in Muhammad's Medina. A woman was the designated cutter. The Prophet, rather than prohibiting the practice, gave procedural guidance: don't cut too deeply, because leaving some tissue is "better for the woman and more liked by the husband."

Why this is a problem

  1. The hadith permits FGM. The Prophet did not forbid the practice. He instructed the practitioner to do it less severely. The jurisprudential weight of this single passage has been enormous in Shafi'i and Shafi'i-influenced traditions.
  2. The stated rationale centers the husband's pleasure. "More liked by the husband" is one of two reasons given. A woman's body is being cut, and a key stated reason for doing it less severely is spousal preference.
  3. Abu Dawud grades it weak — but preserved it anyway. Weak hadiths can support recommended practices in Islamic jurisprudence, and this one did. The grading does not insulate women from the consequences of the ruling.
  4. The scope of impact is massive. UNICEF estimates approximately 200 million women alive today have undergone FGM. A significant fraction are Muslim, and the tradition cites this hadith as textual cover.

The Muslim response

Modern Muslim scholars broadly classify FGM as prohibited or at most a pre-Islamic custom with no binding Islamic basis, citing the hadith's weak chain and the general principle against causing harm. The "do not go to extremes" is read as effectively discouraging the practice entirely; many contemporary scholars hold that the Prophet's instruction was a limiting intervention intended to phase out the practice.

Why it fails

The moral test of a prophet is whether, confronted with the cutting of girls' genitals, he forbade the practice or regulated it. The text records regulation — with the husband's pleasure as a stated reason for moderation. The "discouraging the practice" reading requires reversing the plain meaning: "do not go to extremes" is not the same as "do not do it." Shafi'i jurisprudence and Egyptian Azharite scholars historically read this as obligatory or recommended — not as a discouragement — and their reading is closer to the plain text. The chain's weakness did not prevent fourteen centuries of operational application. Over 200 million women are the evidence that the text was not read as a soft discouragement.

The Quran was revealed in seven variant readings Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #1475-#1478
"This Qur'an has been revealed in seven Ahruf, so recite whatever is convenient of it."

What the hadith says

The Quran was revealed in seven different variant readings. Reciters could use any variant; divine-name endings could be swapped between readings.

Why this is a problem

  1. "One perfectly preserved Quran" cannot coexist with "seven revealed variants." The preservation claim requires a single definitive text; the ahruf hadith concedes there were seven equally valid ones.
  2. Uthman burned six of seven God-given readings. The Uthmanic standardization destroyed readings that, per this hadith, were themselves divinely revealed. A human caliph edited the Quran.
  3. Ibn Mas'ud and Ubayy ibn Ka'b had different Qurans — both certified by Muhammad. The tradition records Muhammad explicitly praising multiple reciters with variant texts. The canonical single-Quran claim erases this.

The Muslim response

Classical scholarship argues the seven ahruf were dialectal and phonetic variants permitted for ease of recitation, not substantially different texts. Uthman's standardization preserved the most widely attested reading and eliminated variant-based confusion — a preservationist act, not an editorial one. The modern seven qira'at (canonical recitation styles) are the legitimate surviving expression of this diversity.

Why it fails

The "phonetic-dialectal variants only" reading is a post-Uthmanic apologetic that classical scholars themselves disagreed on — the range of what counts as an "ahruf" variant has never been settled. Ibn Mas'ud's Quran lacked two complete surahs (113 and 114) that Uthman's standard text includes; that is not phonetic variation. A human caliph making the final selection among revealed variants, destroying the others, is not a preservationist act by any definition that takes "revealed" seriously. The preservation guarantee of Q 15:9 either means the six destroyed variants were not preserved — in which case the guarantee failed — or they were not truly revealed, in which case the hadith's "seven ahruf" is wrong.

A blind man killed his slave-concubine for cursing Muhammad — no retaliation Prophetic Character Violence Women Strong Abu Dawud #4361
"He took a dagger, placed it on her belly, pressed it, and killed her... The Prophet said: 'Oh be witness, no retaliation is payable for her blood.'"

What the hadith says

A blind Muslim killed his slave-concubine — the mother of his children — for insulting Muhammad. Muhammad declared no blood-money was owed and no retaliation was due. The ruling became the canonical foundation for blasphemy-killing law.

Why this is a problem

  1. It is the canonical foundation of blasphemy-death jurisprudence. The principle that insulting the Prophet removes legal protection of the offender's blood — and that the killer faces no legal consequence — traces directly to this hadith.
  2. The victim was doubly vulnerable. Enslaved and female, the woman had no legal standing to defend herself. Her dual vulnerability made her legally disposable under the ruling Muhammad affirmed.
  3. She was the mother of his children — killed in bed. The hadith records she was pregnant with his child or the mother of his children. The intimacy makes the killing more striking, not less — and Muhammad's absolution of it is the canonical record's contribution.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics frames this as a specific ruling on blasphemy — a category Islam treats with exceptional severity because it constitutes a public attack on the community's foundational claims. The absence of retaliation is read as reflecting the seriousness of the offense rather than as a general license to kill vulnerable people.

Why it fails

The "exceptional severity of blasphemy" framing is exactly the problem, not a defense. A ruling that removes legal protection from an enslaved mother because she verbally insulted someone has placed the Prophet's honor above a human life — and specifically above the life of someone with no legal recourse to defend herself. The canonical record is that Muhammad heard about a man killing his sleeping slave-concubine and said: no consequence. Fourteen centuries of blasphemy-killing jurisprudence in multiple jurisdictions follows from that ruling. The "specific case" framing does not confine it; it established it as precedent.
